subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. The device you are reading this on, the air you are breathing, the water in the well or tap near your home, the goat milk cooling in a clay pot, the salt sprinkled on your bariis - every single one of these is made of matter. And here is one of the most powerful ideas in all of science: even though these things look completely different, they are all built from unbelievably tiny pieces called particles. In this unit you will learn to see the world the way a scientist does, imagining the invisible particles that make up everything, and using that idea to explain things you have seen your whole life but perhaps never questioned.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the following. Keep this list close, and tick each item off when you are confident.
Describe the three states of matter - solid, liquid and gas - in terms of the arrangement, movement and energy of their particles.
Use the particle model to explain everyday properties, such as why solids keep their shape, why liquids flow and take the shape of their container, and why gases spread out to fill any space.
Explain changes of state (melting, freezing, evaporation, boiling, condensation and sublimation) in terms of particles gaining or losing energy.
Describe the water cycle and connect it to the seasonal rains of East Africa, using the particle model to explain evaporation and condensation.
Define atoms and elements, and explain that an element is made of only one type of atom.
Distinguish between an element, a compound and a mixture, and give local examples of each.
Explain how mixtures can be separated using methods such as filtration, evaporation and distillation.
Apply these ideas to real situations, such as separating salt from seawater or cleaning muddy water for safe drinking.

Before we build our understanding, study this table of the most important words in the unit. You will meet each of them again and again. Read the example in the third column carefully - it links the science to things you already know.

concepts.title: Why does a puddle on a road near Mombasa disappear after…
concepts.explanation: Why does a puddle on a road near Mombasa disappear after the sun comes out? Why does steam rise from a pot of boiling water when your mother prepares tea? Why do the Gu and Deyr rains fall from clouds that were once ocean water? Why can you dissolve sugar in water but not sand? Why does the sea taste salty, and how do coastal families near the Indian Ocean turn seawater into pure white salt? By the end of this unit, you will be able to answer every one of these questions using one beautifully simple model: the…
concepts.example: This unit also takes you deeper than you may have gone before. You will meet atoms, the smallest particles of all, and learn how they join to build elements, compounds, and mixtures. Understanding these ideas is the…

concepts.title: This unit also takes you deeper than you may have gone…
concepts.explanation: This unit also takes you deeper than you may have gone before. You will meet atoms, the smallest particles of all, and learn how they join to build elements, compounds, and mixtures. Understanding these ideas is the doorway to all of chemistry. The engineers who design water filters for villages, the doctors who prepare medicines, the farmers who understand their soil, and the builders who choose the right materials - all of them rely on the ideas you are about to master. As Muslims we are taught to look closely…
concepts.example: Did You Know? A single drop of water contains more water particles (molecules) than there are stars in the entire visible universe. If you could count one particle every second, it would take you far longer than a human…

concepts.title: Did You Know? A single drop of water contains more water…
concepts.explanation: Did You Know? A single drop of water contains more water particles (molecules) than there are stars in the entire visible universe. If you could count one particle every second, it would take you far longer than a human lifetime to count the particles in that one drop. That is how astonishingly small particles are - and why we could not see them for most of human history.

concepts.example: Year 7 - Unit 2 | The Particle Model, States of Matter, and the Building Blocks of Everything

explorations.title: Watching Diffusion: Do Particles Really Move?
explorations.context: To find out whether particles move on their own, and to see how temperature affects how fast they move.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: What do you think will happen when a drop of colour is placed in still water? Will it spread faster in hot water or cold water? Why?
explorations.answer: Did the colour spread even though you did not stir? What does this tell you about particles? In which glass did the colour spread faster? Why do you think that is? How does this experiment support the idea that heating gives particles more energy?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether particles move on their own, and to see how temperature affects how fast they move.

explorations.title: The Journey of Water: Changes of State
explorations.context: To observe two changes of state - evaporation and condensation - and connect them to the water cycle.
explorations.prompt: Write down: What will happen to the water level in an uncovered dish left in the sun over several days? And what will you see form on a cool surface held above warm water?
explorations.answer: What happened to the water level in the dish over three days? Where did the water go? Name the change of state. What formed on the cold spoon? Where did it come from? Name the change of state. How do these two parts together model the water cycle over the Indian Ocean and the coming of the Gu rains?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe two changes of state - evaporation and condensation - and connect them to the water cycle.

explorations.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
explorations.context: To separate an insoluble solid (soil) from water using filtration, and to see what filtration can and cannot remove.
explorations.prompt: Write down: Will filtering remove ALL the impurities from muddy water and make it safe to drink? Why or why not?
explorations.answer: What was trapped on the cloth? Why did it not pass through? The filtered water is clearer, but is it safe to drink straight away? Explain why filtration alone does not remove germs. Why can filtration separate sand from water, but NOT salt from water?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To separate an insoluble solid (soil) from water using filtration, and to see what filtration can and cannot remove.

explorations.title: Making Salt from Salt Water (Evaporation)
explorations.context: To separate a dissolved solid (salt) from water by evaporation, copying how coastal families harvest sea salt.
explorations.prompt: Write down: When the salty water dries up, what will be left behind, and where will the water have gone?
explorations.answer: What was left in the dish when the water had gone? What does this prove about whether the salt 'disappeared' when it dissolved? Where did the water go? Name the change of state and describe it using particles. How is your experiment the same as the salt pans on the Indian Ocean coast? What does the…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To separate a dissolved solid (salt) from water by evaporation, copying how coastal families harvest sea salt.

explorations.title: Squashing the States: Are Gases Different?
explorations.context: To compare how easily a solid, a liquid and a gas can be squashed, and to link this to how far apart their particles are.
explorations.prompt: Write down: Which will be easiest to squash - a solid, a liquid or a gas? Put them in order and explain your reasoning using particles.
explorations.answer: Which was easiest to squash, and which was hardest? Put the three states in order. Use the particle model to explain your results. Why can a gas be squashed but a liquid and a solid cannot? How does this connect to pumping air into a bicycle or football, but not being able to 'pump' water into a…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To compare how easily a solid, a liquid and a gas can be squashed, and to link this to how far apart their particles are.

methods.title: Watching Diffusion: Do Particles Really Move?
methods.example: To find out whether particles move on their own, and to see how temperature affects how fast they move.
steps: Fill one glass with cold water and the other with the same amount of warm water. Let them sit still for one minute so the water stops moving.
Gently place a single drop of your colour into the centre of each glass at the same time. Do this as gently as you can.
Do NOT stir. Watch closely and do not bump the table.
Observe how the colour spreads in each glass. Note the time it takes to spread evenly through each glass.
Record your observations in the table below.

methods.title: The Journey of Water: Changes of State
methods.example: To observe two changes of state - evaporation and condensation - and connect them to the water cycle.
steps: Part A - Evaporation: Fill the shallow dish with water. Mark the starting level with the marker or place a stone at the edge of the water line. Leave the dish in a sunny, safe place.
Check the water level each day for three days and record it. Do not add any water.
Part B - Condensation: Ask an adult to help. Hold the chilled metal spoon a short, safe distance ABOVE (not touching) a cup of warm water for about one minute.
Look closely at the underside of the cold spoon. Record what you see forming on it.
Observation Recording Sheet

methods.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
methods.example: To separate an insoluble solid (soil) from water using filtration, and to see what filtration can and cannot remove.
steps: Make muddy water by stirring a spoon of soil or sand into a jar of water.
Let it stand for a minute and observe. Notice that heavier bits sink (this is called sedimentation).
Fold the cloth several times and place it over the mouth of the empty container, pushing it slightly into the opening to make a dip.
Slowly pour the muddy water through the cloth into the container.
Compare the water that passed through (the filtrate) with what was left on the cloth (the residue).

methods.title: Making Salt from Salt Water (Evaporation)
methods.example: To separate a dissolved solid (salt) from water by evaporation, copying how coastal families harvest sea salt.
steps: Stir salt into warm water, a little at a time, until no more will dissolve. You have made a salt solution.
Observe that the water looks clear - the salt has not disappeared, it has dissolved and spread through the water.
Pour a thin layer of the solution into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a warm, sunny spot and leave it undisturbed for one to several days.
Check each day. When the water has fully evaporated, examine what is left in the dish.

methods.title: Squashing the States: Are Gases Different?
methods.example: To compare how easily a solid, a liquid and a gas can be squashed, and to link this to how far apart their particles are.
steps: Pull the plunger back so the syringe is full of air. Seal the tip firmly with your finger.
Push the plunger. Notice how far it moves and how the air pushes back. Record what you feel.
Now fill the syringe with water, remove all air bubbles, and seal the tip with your finger.
Try to push the plunger again. Record how far it moves compared with the air.
(Optional) Consider a solid object: imagine trying to squash a pebble by squeezing it. Record your reasoning.

workedExamples.title: Separating Salt, Sand and Water
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through the problem from the callout together, step by step, the way a scientist would. Problem: Amina has a jar containing sand, salt and water all mixed. She wants to end up with three separate things: dry sand, dry salt, and clean water. How…
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Think about the properties. Sand is an insoluble solid (it does not dissolve). Salt is a soluble solid (it dissolves). Water is the liquid. Each property points to a method. Step 2 - Filter to remove the sand. Amina pours the mixture through a filter cloth. The sand is trapped as residue on the cloth. What passes through is salty water (the filtrate), because the dissolved salt is too small to be trapped. She now has wet sand and a jar of salty water. Step 3 - Dry the sand. Amina spreads the wet sand in…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: What happens to particles when a substance is heated? (a) they gain energy and move faster (b) they lose energy (c) they stop moving (d) they disappear
workedExamples.solution: (a) they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Changing from a liquid to a gas at a temperature below boiling point is called: (a) condensation (b) melting (c) evaporation (d) freezing
workedExamples.solution: (c) evaporation. Evaporation happens at the surface, below boiling point; boiling happens throughout at the boiling point.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The white clouds that form before the Gu rains are made when water vapour: (a) melts (b) condenses into tiny droplets (c) freezes into ice (d) is destroyed
workedExamples.solution: (b) condenses into tiny droplets. Rising water vapour cools, loses energy, and condenses into the droplets that form clouds.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A substance made of only one type of atom is called: (a) a compound (b) a mixture (c) an element (d) a solution
workedExamples.solution: (c) an element. An element contains only one type of atom, such as oxygen or iron.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a compound? (a) air (b) water (H2O) (c) seawater (d) sweet tea
workedExamples.solution: (b) water (H2O). Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined - the others are mixtures.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a mixture? (a) oxygen (b) pure iron (c) seawater (d) table salt
workedExamples.solution: (c) seawater. Seawater is water with dissolved salts mixed in; the others are pure substances.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: To separate an insoluble solid such as sand from water, you would use: (a) evaporation (b) filtration (c) distillation (d) freezing
workedExamples.solution: (b) filtration. Filtration traps insoluble solids like sand while the liquid passes through.

practice.prompt: In which state of matter are the particles held in a fixed, regular pattern and only able to vibrate? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) none of these
practice.answer: (a) solid. In a solid, particles are locked in a regular pattern and can only vibrate on the spot.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What happens to particles when a substance is heated? (a) they gain energy and move faster (b) they lose energy (c) they stop moving (d) they disappear
practice.answer: (a) they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Changing from a liquid to a gas at a temperature below boiling point is called: (a) condensation (b) melting (c) evaporation (d) freezing
practice.answer: (c) evaporation. Evaporation happens at the surface, below boiling point; boiling happens throughout at the boiling point.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The white clouds that form before the Gu rains are made when water vapour: (a) melts (b) condenses into tiny droplets (c) freezes into ice (d) is destroyed
practice.answer: (b) condenses into tiny droplets. Rising water vapour cools, loses energy, and condenses into the droplets that form clouds.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A substance made of only one type of atom is called: (a) a compound (b) a mixture (c) an element (d) a solution
practice.answer: (c) an element. An element contains only one type of atom, such as oxygen or iron.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a compound? (a) air (b) water (H2O) (c) seawater (d) sweet tea
practice.answer: (b) water (H2O). Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined - the others are mixtures.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a mixture? (a) oxygen (b) pure iron (c) seawater (d) table salt
practice.answer: (c) seawater. Seawater is water with dissolved salts mixed in; the others are pure substances.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To separate an insoluble solid such as sand from water, you would use: (a) evaporation (b) filtration (c) distillation (d) freezing
practice.answer: (b) filtration. Filtration traps insoluble solids like sand while the liquid passes through.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Coastal families harvest salt from seawater mainly by: (a) filtration (b) evaporation (c) distillation (d) condensation
practice.answer: (b) evaporation. The sun evaporates the water, leaving solid salt behind in the pans.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: While ice is melting, its temperature: (a) rises steadily (b) falls (c) stays the same until all the ice has melted (d) doubles
practice.answer: (c) stays the same until all the ice has melted. The energy is used to break forces between particles, not to raise temperature.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which statement about particles in a gas is correct? (a) they are packed tightly (b) they are far apart and move quickly in all directions (c) they cannot move (d) they have no mass
practice.answer: (b) they are far apart and move quickly in all directions. This is the defining feature of a gas.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The chemical symbol for oxygen is: (a) O (b) Ox (c) Og (d) Oy
practice.answer: (a) O. O is the internationally used chemical symbol for oxygen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Watching Diffusion: Do Particles Really Move?
activities.materials: Two clear glasses or jars of the same size; Cold water (from the fridge or a cool well) and hot water (warm from a kettle, not boiling); A few drops of a strong colour: tea, coffee, food colouring, or juice from crushed dark berries or beetroot; A spoon (but only for the colour - do NOT stir the…
steps: Fill one glass with cold water and the other with the same amount of warm water. Let them sit still for one minute so the water stops moving.
Gently place a single drop of your colour into the centre of each glass at the same time. Do this as gently as you can.
Do NOT stir. Watch closely and do not bump the table.
Observe how the colour spreads in each glass. Note the time it takes to spread evenly through each glass.
Record your observations in the table below.

activities.title: The Journey of Water: Changes of State
activities.materials: A shallow dish or plate; Water; A marker or a small stone to mark the starting water level; A metal spoon or a metal lid, chilled in the fridge; A cup of warm (not boiling) water
steps: Part A - Evaporation: Fill the shallow dish with water. Mark the starting level with the marker or place a stone at the edge of the water line. Leave the dish in a sunny, safe place.
Check the water level each day for three days and record it. Do not add any water.
Part B - Condensation: Ask an adult to help. Hold the chilled metal spoon a short, safe distance ABOVE (not touching) a cup of warm water for about one minute.
Look closely at the underside of the cold spoon. Record what you see forming on it.
Observation Recording Sheet

activities.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
activities.materials: A jar or bottle; Water mixed with clean garden soil or sand to make 'muddy water'; A clean cloth (such as a folded piece of an old cotton shirt) or a coffee filter / paper towel; A second clean container to catch the filtered water; A rubber band or string to hold the cloth (optional)
steps: Make muddy water by stirring a spoon of soil or sand into a jar of water.
Let it stand for a minute and observe. Notice that heavier bits sink (this is called sedimentation).
Fold the cloth several times and place it over the mouth of the empty container, pushing it slightly into the opening to make a dip.
Slowly pour the muddy water through the cloth into the container.
Compare the water that passed through (the filtrate) with what was left on the cloth (the residue).

activities.title: Making Salt from Salt Water (Evaporation)
activities.materials: Warm water; Table salt; A spoon for stirring; A dark-coloured shallow dish or plate (dark surfaces absorb more heat); A sunny windowsill or safe outdoor spot
steps: Stir salt into warm water, a little at a time, until no more will dissolve. You have made a salt solution.
Observe that the water looks clear - the salt has not disappeared, it has dissolved and spread through the water.
Pour a thin layer of the solution into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a warm, sunny spot and leave it undisturbed for one to several days.
Check each day. When the water has fully evaporated, examine what is left in the dish.

activities.title: Squashing the States: Are Gases Different?
activities.materials: An empty plastic syringe with NO needle (such as a medicine syringe) - or a bicycle pump with the outlet blocked by a finger; A small solid object that fits (like a pebble or a bean) - optional; Water; Air (already in the syringe)
steps: Pull the plunger back so the syringe is full of air. Seal the tip firmly with your finger.
Push the plunger. Notice how far it moves and how the air pushes back. Record what you feel.
Now fill the syringe with water, remove all air bubbles, and seal the tip with your finger.
Try to push the plunger again. Record how far it moves compared with the air.
(Optional) Consider a solid object: imagine trying to squash a pebble by squeezing it. Record your reasoning.

terms: Liquid to gas at the surface, below boiling point.
Liquid to gas throughout, at the boiling point.
commonMistakes: Saying particles in a solid do not move
Particles are never still - they always have energy
Matter cannot be destroyed, only changed
Calling the white cloud above a pot 'steam'
True steam is an invisible gas
They join in completely different ways

fluency.prompt: In which state of matter are the particles held in a fixed, regular pattern and only able to vibrate? (a) solid (b) liquid (c) gas (d) none of these
fluency.answer: (a) solid. In a solid, particles are locked in a regular pattern and can only vibrate on the spot.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (a) solid. In a solid, particles are locked in a regular pattern and can only vibrate on the spot.

fluency.prompt: What happens to particles when a substance is heated? (a) they gain energy and move faster (b) they lose energy (c) they stop moving (d) they disappear
fluency.answer: (a) they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (a) they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.

fluency.prompt: Changing from a liquid to a gas at a temperature below boiling point is called: (a) condensation (b) melting (c) evaporation (d) freezing
fluency.answer: (c) evaporation. Evaporation happens at the surface, below boiling point; boiling happens throughout at the boiling point.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) evaporation. Evaporation happens at the surface, below boiling point; boiling happens throughout at the boiling point.

fluency.prompt: The white clouds that form before the Gu rains are made when water vapour: (a) melts (b) condenses into tiny droplets (c) freezes into ice (d) is destroyed
fluency.answer: (b) condenses into tiny droplets. Rising water vapour cools, loses energy, and condenses into the droplets that form clouds.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) condenses into tiny droplets. Rising water vapour cools, loses energy, and condenses into the droplets that form clouds.

fluency.prompt: A substance made of only one type of atom is called: (a) a compound (b) a mixture (c) an element (d) a solution

fluency.answer: (c) seawater. Seawater is water with dissolved salts mixed in; the others are pure substances.

fluency.answer: (b) filtration. Filtration traps insoluble solids like sand while the liquid passes through.

fluency.answer: (c) stays the same until all the ice has melted. The energy is used to break forces between particles, not to raise temperature.

fluency.answer: (b) they are far apart and move quickly in all directions. This is the defining feature of a gas.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

realProblems.prompt: Apply what you have learned to real situations.
realProblems.answer: Step 1: let the water stand so heavy mud sinks (sedimentation). Step 2: filter it through clean cloth to trap the mud and grit (this removes the insoluble solids but not germs). Step 3: boil the filtered water to kill germs and make it safe to drink. Each step removes a different problem:…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Step 1: let the water stand so heavy mud sinks (sedimentation). Step 2: filter it through clean cloth to trap the mud and grit (this removes the insoluble solids but not germs). Step 3: boil the filtered water to kill germs and make it safe to drink. Each step removes a different problem:…

realProblems.prompt: Amina fetches water from a river and it is cloudy with mud. Describe, step by step, how she could make it clearer and then safe to drink. Name the methods and explain why each step is needed.
realProblems.answer: The water in the tea evaporated in the heat (a liquid-to-gas change of state), so the level dropped. The tea was a mixture; when the water left, the dissolved sugar could not evaporate, so it was left behind as the sticky layer - just like salt is left when seawater evaporates.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The water in the tea evaporated in the heat (a liquid-to-gas change of state), so the level dropped. The tea was a mixture; when the water left, the dissolved sugar could not evaporate, so it was left behind as the sticky layer - just like salt is left when seawater evaporates.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan leaves a cup of sweet tea outside on a hot day and forgets it. When he returns, the tea level has dropped and there is a sticky sugary layer at the bottom. Explain what happened using changes of state and the idea of a mixture.
realProblems.answer: The disrupted stage is precipitation (rainfall) - and behind it, reduced evaporation/condensation over the region so clouds do not form or do not reach the land. Without rain, wells and rivers are not refilled, crops fail and animals have no water, so the whole farming and herding community suffers.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The disrupted stage is precipitation (rainfall) - and behind it, reduced evaporation/condensation over the region so clouds do not form or do not reach the land. Without rain, wells and rivers are not refilled, crops fail and animals have no water, so the whole farming and herding community suffers.

realProblems.prompt: During a long drought the wells run dry and the rains do not come. Using the water cycle, explain which stage has been disrupted and why the whole community is affected.
realProblems.answer: They should use distillation. They boil the salty water so the water turns to steam (leaving the salt behind), then lead the steam to a cool surface where it condenses back into pure liquid water, which is collected. The salt stays in the original container.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: They should use distillation. They boil the salty water so the water turns to steam (leaving the salt behind), then lead the steam to a cool surface where it condenses back into pure liquid water, which is collected. The salt stays in the original container.

realProblems.prompt: A family living far from the coast wants to turn salty water into fresh drinking water. Which separation method should they use, and how does it work? Explain step by step.
realProblems.answer: The water was not destroyed. Boiling gave the water particles enough energy to escape as a gas (water vapour/steam) into the air - a change of state from liquid to gas. The same particles still exist in the air; matter is conserved, not destroyed.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The water was not destroyed. Boiling gave the water particles enough energy to escape as a gas (water vapour/steam) into the air - a change of state from liquid to gas. The same particles still exist in the air; matter is conserved, not destroyed.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf says, 'When my mother boils the pot dry, the water is destroyed.' Explain to Yusuf, using the particle model, why he is wrong and what really happened to the water.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the arrangement and movement of particles in a liquid.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: In a liquid the particles are close together but not in a fixed pattern; they can slide and roll past one another, which lets the liquid flow and take the shape of its container.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using particles, why a gas can be squashed but a solid cannot.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: In a gas the particles are far apart with large empty spaces, so they can be pushed closer together (squashed). In a solid the particles are already touching with almost no gaps, so there is no room to squash them.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the change of state when steam turns into water droplets on a cold surface, and say whether energy is gained or lost.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: This is condensation, and energy is lost (given out) as the gas cools and the particles slow down and join together.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the temperature stay the same while water is boiling, even though heat is still being added?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The temperature stays the same because all the added heat energy is being used to break the forces holding the particles together (turning liquid to gas), not to make the particles move faster or hotter.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between an element and a compound, giving one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An element is made of only one type of atom (for example, oxygen or iron). A compound is two or more elements chemically joined (for example, water, H2O). The key difference is that a compound contains more than one element bonded together.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two everyday examples of mixtures found in a Somali or East African home.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Accept any two reasonable mixtures, such as: sweet tea (water, tea, sugar), seawater, air, soil, sand and water, or camel milk with added water. Each keeps the properties of its parts and can be separated.

questions.question: In which state of matter are the particles held in a fixed, regular pattern and only able to vibrate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solid. In a solid, particles are locked in a regular pattern and can only vibrate on the spot.

questions.question: What happens to particles when a substance is heated?
options: they gain energy and move faster
questions.answer: they gain energy and move faster
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.

questions.question: Changing from a liquid to a gas at a temperature below boiling point is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: evaporation. Evaporation happens at the surface, below boiling point; boiling happens throughout at the boiling point.

questions.question: The white clouds that form before the Gu rains are made when water vapour:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: condenses into tiny droplets. Rising water vapour cools, loses energy, and condenses into the droplets that form clouds.

questions.question: A substance made of only one type of atom is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an element. An element contains only one type of atom, such as oxygen or iron.

questions.question: Which of these is a compound?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: water (H2O). Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined - the others are mixtures.

questions.question: Which of these is a mixture?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: seawater. Seawater is water with dissolved salts mixed in; the others are pure substances.

questions.question: To separate an insoluble solid such as sand from water, you would use:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: filtration. Filtration traps insoluble solids like sand while the liquid passes through.

questions.question: Coastal families harvest salt from seawater mainly by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: evaporation. The sun evaporates the water, leaving solid salt behind in the pans.

questions.question: While ice is melting, its temperature:
options: stays the same until all the ice has melted
questions.answer: stays the same until all the ice has melted
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stays the same until all the ice has melted. The energy is used to break forces between particles, not to raise temperature.

questions.question: Which statement about particles in a gas is correct?
options: they are far apart and move quickly in all directions
questions.answer: they are far apart and move quickly in all directions
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: they are far apart and move quickly in all directions. This is the defining feature of a gas.

questions.question: The chemical symbol for oxygen is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: O. O is the internationally used chemical symbol for oxygen.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the following. Keep this list close, and tick each item off when you are confident.
I can describe the three states of matter - solid, liquid and gas - in terms of the arrangement, movement and energy of their particles.
I can use the particle model to explain everyday properties, such as why solids keep their shape, why liquids flow and take the shape of their container, and why gases spread out to fill any space.
I can explain changes of state (melting, freezing, evaporation, boiling, condensation and sublimation) in terms of particles gaining or losing energy.
I can describe the water cycle and connect it to the seasonal rains of East Africa, using the particle model to explain evaporation and condensation.
I can define atoms and elements, and explain that an element is made of only one type of atom.
I can distinguish between an element, a compound and a mixture, and give local examples of each.
I can explain how mixtures can be separated using methods such as filtration, evaporation and distillation.
